s3.q: Postterm pregnancy is defined as a pregnancy continuing beyond:
s3.explain: Postterm pregnancy is a pregnancy continuing beyond 42 weeks. Pregnancy between 41 to 42 weeks is called prolonged pregnancy.

s3.q: A pregnancy between 41 to 42 weeks is called:
s3.explain: Pregnancy between 41 to 42 weeks is called prolonged pregnancy, while beyond 42 weeks is postterm pregnancy.

s3.q: What is the key difference between prolonged and postterm pregnancy?
options: There is no difference — the terms are interchangeable
Prolonged is 41-42 weeks (monitored); postterm is >42 weeks (requires intervention)
Prolonged requires induction; postterm is observed expectantly
Prolonged has higher fetal risk than postterm
s3.explain: Prolonged pregnancy (41-42 weeks) is monitored; postterm pregnancy (>42 weeks) requires intervention. Do not confuse the two — management differs.

s4.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s4.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s4.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s4.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s4.q: The most common cause of postterm pregnancy is:
s4.explain: The most common cause of postterm pregnancy is wrong date, so a careful review of menstrual history is important in all such cases.

s4.q: Which fetal condition can cause postterm pregnancy?
s4.explain: Fetal factors causing postterm pregnancy include anencephaly and adrenal hypoplasia (diminished fetal cortisol response). Anencephaly disrupts the fetal hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis — without fetal cortisol, labor is not initiated.

s4.q: Maternal factors associated with postterm pregnancy include all EXCEPT:
s4.explain: Maternal factors include primipara, history of previous prolonged pregnancy, and sedentary life. Multiple gestation is associated with preterm labor, not postterm pregnancy.

s5.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s5.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: Which of the following is NOT an antenatal ultrasound finding in postterm pregnancy?
s5.explain: Antenatal ultrasound findings in postterm pregnancy include BPD > 9.6 cm, increased fetal weight, oligohydramnios, increased placental calcification, and Doppler evidence of placental insufficiency.

s5.q: Increased placental calcification on ultrasound in postterm pregnancy reflects:
s5.explain: Increased placental calcification on US is a hallmark of placental aging — it reflects the pathophysiology of postterm pregnancy where the placenta becomes insufficient.

s5.q: Oligohydramnios in a postterm pregnancy poses risk for all EXCEPT:
s5.explain: Oligohydramnios + placental insufficiency in a postterm pregnancy = high risk for cord compression, fetal hypoxia, and meconium aspiration. Fetal macrosomia is a separate complication of postterm pregnancy.

s6.q: A postnatal sign of a postterm baby is:
options: Skin that is thin and transparent
s6.explain: Postnatal signs of a postterm baby include weight more than 4.5 kg, length more than 54 cm, well ossified skull with smaller fontanelles, and finger nails projecting beyond finger tips.

s6.q: Which mnemonic helps recall the postnatal signs of a postterm baby?
s6.explain: L-W-S-F: Length > 54 cm, Weight > 4.5 kg, Skull well ossified/small fontanelles, Fingernails beyond tips.

s6.q: A postterm baby's skull is characterized by:
s6.explain: The skull is well ossified with smaller fontanelles in a postterm baby. (Spalding sign — overlapping skull bones — is seen in IUFD, not postterm.)

s7.q: The cascade of complications in postterm pregnancy begins with:
options: Aging of placenta leading to placental insufficiency
s7.explain: The cascade: placental aging → placental insufficiency → fetal hypoxia → acidosis → stillbirth. Each day past 42 weeks increases this risk.

s7.q: Fetal macrosomia in postterm pregnancy increases the risk of:
s7.explain: Fetal macrosomia leads to shoulder dystocia, increases the chances of birth trauma (intracranial hemorrhage) and increases the chances of operative delivery.

s7.q: Complications of postterm pregnancy include all EXCEPT:
s7.explain: Pulmonary hypoplasia is a complication of preterm PROM, not postterm pregnancy. Postterm complications include placental insufficiency, fetal hypoxia/acidosis/stillbirth, oligohydramnios, cord compression, meconium aspiration, fetal macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, operative delivery, postpartum hemorrhage, and infection.

s8.q: The clinical significance of this section is:

s8.q: The management of postterm pregnancy is:
options: Termination of pregnancy (induction of labor or cesarean)
s8.explain: The source states management of postterm pregnancy is termination of pregnancy — induction of labor or cesarean section depending on cervical readiness and fetal status.

s8.q: The decision between induction of labor and cesarean section in postterm pregnancy depends on:
s8.explain: Management depends on cervical readiness and fetal status, and the type of termination (induction of labor or cesarean section) is individualized.

s8.q: Why must dating be confirmed before diagnosing postterm pregnancy?
options: Because management differs significantly between true postterm and wrong dates
Because ultrasound is unreliable after 40 weeks
Because the definition changes by country
Because postterm cannot be diagnosed without a first-trimester scan
s8.explain: The most common cause of postterm pregnancy is wrong date, so careful review of menstrual history and dating confirmation is essential before making the diagnosis.
